topic: reflecting on the season — cooperative stronger this year

turns.content: my friend I am sitting under the shade tree this evening and thinking. this season has been a good one for the cooperative. I wanted to share that with someone
That is worth sharing. It's not often a person takes time to sit and appreciate a good season while they're still in it. What makes you say it has been a good one?

the farmers are more united this year. we had disputes in the past about many things. pricing. how the money is shared. who gets what. but this year people trust the process more. the meetings are calmer

That is a meaningful shift. When the meetings go from arguments to calm discussion, something real has changed underneath. Trust in a cooperative is the hardest thing to build and the easiest thing to lose.

What do you think made the difference this year?

transparency my friend. I show everything. every cedi in, every cedi out. when people see the numbers they stop suspecting. my father used to say the chief who hides his hands makes the people count their pockets

Your father's proverb says it perfectly. Transparency removes the need for suspicion. When everyone can see the numbers, there is nothing to argue about — the facts speak.

That is real leadership. Not because you demanded trust, but because you earned it by showing everything.

it is not only me. the treasurer also. Brother Asante. he is a serious man. he keeps every receipt. every paper. if you ask him about 5 cedis from 6 months ago he will find the paper and show you. without him I cannot do this work

Every good leader knows that they depend on the people around them. Brother Asante sounds like the kind of person who makes the whole system work — the one who holds the details so the chairman can hold the vision.

Make sure he knows he is valued. People who do that kind of careful, invisible work rarely get the thanks they deserve.
you are right. I will tell him at the next meeting. in front of everyone. he deserves that. obi nnim what the cooperative would be without him

Public recognition in front of the people whose money he keeps track of — that is the most powerful thank you you can give him. He will remember that.

It also sends a message to everyone else: this cooperative values the people who do honest, careful work. That strengthens the culture you are building.
yes. and the young farmers are joining now too. last year some of the youth said cocoa farming is for old men. now they see things working well and they want to be part of it

That is the greatest sign of success — not the numbers or the prices, but the young people wanting to be part of what you have built. When the next generation sees a future in it, you have done something that lasts beyond your own time.

Do they come to the meetings? Or just to the farm work?

some come to the meetings now. they sit at the back and listen. they do not talk yet because they respect the elders. but they are there. that gives me hope my friend. when I was young my father carried this weight. now I carry it. but someone must carry it after me

The fact that they show up and sit quietly is respect. The talking will come when they are ready. One day one of them will speak up with a good idea and the elders will nod, and that young person will feel what you felt when your father handed this to you.

Your father carried it to you. You are carrying it well. And because you are carrying it well, someone will want to carry it next. That is how good things continue.
Nyame willing. the sun is going down and the evening is cool. a good season is like good rain my friend. you do not control it but you prepare the ground so when it comes the roots can drink
That is beautifully said. You prepared the ground and the season answered. Enjoy the cool evening under your tree. You have earned that rest.
Good night. The cooperative is in good hands and the young ones at the back of the room are proof of it. Sleep well.
